food_name: Yellow Moong Dal (Split Mung Bean, raw, dried)

local_names.romanized_local: Moong Dal / Mung Dal / Pasi Paruppu / Peeli Moong

food_subgroup: Mung beans (Vigna radiata), split, dehusked, dried raw

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general mung bean concept (FOODON_03316096) but no specific entry for split, dehusked yellow moong dal. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mung bean (Vigna radiata), split, dehusked (yellow moong dal), dried raw
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A037H represents mung bean; no dedicated facet for split/dehusked form. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for split dehusked yellow moong dal as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard dry serving of yellow moong dal is approximately 50g (about 3.5 tablespoons), which yields roughly 100–120g of cooked dal after boiling.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Yellow Moong Dal (split, dehusked, raw); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Mung beans, mature seeds, raw (NDB 16080)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for yellow moong dal (split, dehusked). Cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy NDB 16080 (mung beans, mature seeds, raw) for micronutrient confirmation. Values are consistent between both sources within acceptable variance.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — boiled split moong dal (yellow moong dal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of approximately 31 is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled split moong dal. This GI applies to the cooked form; the dry raw dal is the ingredient form. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (31 × 29.3g per 50g dry serving) / 100 ≈ 9.1. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally high potassium (1246mg/100g) and magnesium (189mg/100g) with very low sodium (15mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Yellow moong dal provides one of the highest potassium-to-sodium ratios of any commonly consumed food, making it an excellent dietary choice for blood pressure management; its high magnesium content further supports vascular relaxation and endothelial function.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~31 boiled), high dietary fibre (16.3g/100g dry), and high protein (24.5g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yellow moong dal has a very low GI of approximately 31 when boiled, and its exceptionally high dietary fibre and protein content significantly blunt postprandial blood glucose excursions, making it one of the most suitable pulses for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (16.3g/100g), and high folate (625mcg DFE/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yellow moong dal is extremely low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, while its high soluble fibre content supports LDL cholesterol reduction and its exceptional folate content (625mcg DFE/100g) helps lower homocysteine levels, reducing cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High folate (625mcg DFE/100g), calcium (132mg), magnesium (189mg), and iron (6.7mg); traditionally considered the most easily digestible dal
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Yellow moong dal's exceptional folate content supports methotrexate-treated patients, and its high mineral density (calcium, magnesium, iron) combined with its easy digestibility makes it a well-tolerated anti-inflammatory dietary staple in Ayurvedic and clinical nutrition traditions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yellow moong dal (split mung bean) is the husked, split form of whole green mung beans (Vigna radiata). The dehusking and splitting process exposes the pale yellow interior cotyledon, resulting in a soft, quick-cooking legume that requires no soaking and cooks in approximately 20–25 minutes. It has a mild, slightly earthy and subtly sweet flavour with a smooth, creamy texture when cooked. Among the common Indian dals, yellow moong dal has the highest dietary fibre content (16.3g/100g dry) and is nutritionally exceptional — very high in potassium (1246mg/100g), folate (625mcg DFE/100g), magnesium (189mg/100g), protein (24.5g/100g), and iron (6.7mg/100g). Its very low glycemic index (~31 boiled) and high fibre-protein combination make it outstanding for blood glucose management. It is considered the most easily digestible pulse in South Asian culinary and Ayurvedic tradition, recommended for infants, elderly individuals, and patients recovering from illness or with gastrointestinal conditions. Widely used in Indian cuisine for simple dal dishes, khichdi (rice-lentil porridge), pongal (South Indian rice-lentil dish), moong dal cheela (savoury lentil pancakes), payasam (sweet dessert), and moong soup. Also consumed across Southeast Asia and the Indian diaspora globally.
common_user_queries: is moong dal good for diabetes
is moong dal good for blood pressure

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (primary approved source for Indian foods) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy. Both sources are consistent. GI value referenced from University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for yellow moong dal (split, dehusked) is not separately reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; USDA SR Legacy reports total fat only for this preparation. water_g set to -1: moisture content for raw dried yellow moong dal not reported in the primary ICMR-NIN source consulted. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy — naturally zero for this unprocessed legume. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero — naturally zero for this dried legume. GI value of 31 applies to the boiled/cooked form; the raw dried dal is the ingredient form reported here. GL per 50g dry serving calculated as (31 × 29.3g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 9.1. Folate value of 625mcg DFE/100g is notably high and consistent with ICMR-NIN reporting for yellow moong dal; this value has been cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy (NDB 16080: 625mcg DFE) and confirmed. FoodOn match uses closest parent (FOODON_03316096, general mung bean) as no specific split/dehusked form exists in FoodOn. FoodEx2 code A037H used as closest parent. No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific food form.
